title: Mass at the Drive-In Theater
author: https://hedgehogreview.com/contributors/aaron-m-renn
text:
In most accounts of the Catholic Church’s twentieth-century transformation, the Second Vatican Council, with its sweeping changes to liturgy and the role of the laity, is presented as the watershed moment. But other forces were at play as well. One of the most important was the expansion of American suburban life—the steady exodus of people from the cities into what the historian Kenneth Jackson dubbed “the crabgrass frontier.” This demographic trend, beginning in the mid-nineteenth century and accelerating drastically in the early decades after World War II, had profound effects on the American Catholic way of life well before the Second Vatican Council concluded in 1965. That story is the subject of Stephen M. Koeth’s informative new book, Crabgrass Catholicism: How Suburbanization Transformed Faith and Politics in Postwar America.

Koeth’s canvas is a Long Island experiencing suburban hyper-growth on a scale unknown today. In the wake of World War II, GIs returned to marry their sweethearts but, amid a nationwide housing shortage, found few places to live. Due to a housing construction deficit during more than a decade of depression and war, many were forced to double up with family. The suburbs provided the cure: An end to wartime materials rationing and the invention of production-line-like techniques for building homes enabled the rapid proliferation of suburban housing developments such as Long Island’s famous Levittown. Despite their small size and limited amenities, homes in these new suburbs proved enormously popular. 

Pent-up demand and rapidly emerging supply intersected to produce an explosion of suburban growth in the early postwar period. Many American suburbs grew so fast it was as if they sprung fully formed from the brow of Zeus. Nassau County, New York, grew by 93.3 percent between 1950 and 1960, gaining 627,406 people. Suffolk County, New York, grew by 141.5 percent during that same period, adding 390,655 people. This was followed by 69 percent growth the next decade, or 458,166 residents. Today’s biggest suburban growth stories are posting numbers well below these. Collin County, Texas, for example, in the booming North Dallas suburbs, has never added 300,000 people in any decade—this in a county comparable in size to Suffolk and much larger than Nassau. After their growth boom, both Long Island counties essentially flatlined. Nassau County has added fewer than 100,000 people since 1960. Suffolk County added more after 1970—up by 410,959—but this 37 percent growth was spread out over almost fifty-five years.

This growth profile created an impossible challenge for the Catholic Church. The Catholic population on Long Island grew so rapidly that it was impossible to keep up with. But this was followed in quick succession by the challenges of maturity and even decline: Soon enough, the empty fields were all developed, many of those who moved there to raise children stayed on as empty nesters, and the number of children in the next generation declined.

Before the war, American Catholics were largely concentrated in cities. Arriving through Ellis Island in the waves of immigration that reached its climax between 1890 and 1920, they formed strongly ethnic communities, whether in traditional territorial parishes or specifically national ones—Polish, Italian, Irish. Catholicism was thus deeply intertwined with ethnicity. Koeth writes, “In an era of rapid urbanization Catholic immigrants built American city life by fusing the neighborhood with the ethnic parish which was dominated by its priests and religious sisters, centered on its church and school, and bound together by its communal worship and devotions.”

In the suburbs, that old order could no longer hold. Suburban residential patterns were organized more on socioeconomic rather than ethnic lines, against a backdrop of racial exclusion of blacks. “Postwar suburbanization helped complete the amalgamation of various ethnically inflected forms of Catholicism into one religiously identified body of American Catholicism,” Koeth explains. This meant an end to the variegated forms of Catholicism that were ethnic specific in favor of adopting the Irish style of Church. It also meant a shift from thick networks of extended family in the ethnic city neighborhoods to a nuclear-family orientation in the suburbs. According to Koeth, “The suburbs were also comprised of single-family houses in which, especially in the earliest days of suburban development, a single generation of young veterans lived with their wives and children cut off from the extended family networks that long held the ethnic parish together.”

Building Catholic infrastructure to keep up with suburban growth was a major challenge. Money was needed for new land, new churches, and new schools. This was always a challenge, as was staffing, due to a shortage of priests and the religious sisters who traditionally taught in Catholic schools. The difficulties of scaling with growth, as well as the general development patterns of suburbia, led to a landscape of fewer but larger parishes than in the city. Whereas the average size of a city parish had been five hundred families, in the suburbs it was three thousand. Many people argue that the low-density, auto-oriented nature of suburbia is less efficient than cities. But in this case, it enabled a more efficient approach. People could drive to church instead of walking, and there was no longer any need to build a church for every ethnicity. The result was that the Catholic Church needed fewer “points of presence” (to borrow a term from computer networks) in the suburbs.

Even with these new efficiencies, growth was so rapid that it became impossible to build and staff Catholic schools fast enough to serve all the children of the baby boom. That failure to keep up struck at the core of the Catholic model in America: The parish school had been seen as central to transmitting Catholic doctrine and staving off assimilation to Protestant America. Alternative approaches arose, such as CCD (Confraternity of Christian Doctrine) classes, which taught Catholic doctrine to children who didn’t attend parish schools, typically meeting once per week. Eventually, this model came to predominate as the Catholic Church on Long Island, which became its own Diocese of Rockville Centre in 1957, turned away from the parish-school model. Koeth notes, “Of the twenty-one parishes founded after the diocese’s erection in 1957, only one…ever opened a parochial school.”

A shortage of facilities resulted in religious innovations. Priests held Mass in drive-in movie theaters, or even in people’s homes. These might have seemed like canny adaptations to the new rhythms of mid-century life, but they had the unintended effect of detaching people from the parish as the source of religious life, in favor of a more privatized faith. The old ethnic neighborhoods prided themselves on their processions, demonstrating their faith—and ethnicity—in public, but suburban Catholics preferred family devotions at home.

The upheaval was amplified by an instability in parish boundaries that stemmed from the rapid creation of new parishes to keep up with suburban growth. As Koeth writes, “The mobility of suburbanites and the constant shifting of parish boundaries as more and more new parishes were created eroded the territorial meaning of the parish.” Parents complained when they found themselves transferred from an existing parish with a school to a new parish without one: The Church, enforcing parish boundaries strictly in that era, would not allow children to be sent to schools in other parishes.

Greater feedback from the faithful, such as complaints about parish boundary changes, was a sign of growing lay involvement in Church life. Again, some of this was simply unavoidable in a climate of hyper-growth. Koeth observes, “The staggering size of suburban congregations and the diversity of their needs required priests to involve the laity as much as possible in building up the parish.” But the net result was growing lay influence in Church affairs. “Postwar suburbanization accelerated and diversified lay participation in the ministries and administration of the Church and led to a gradual transfer of authority from clergy to laity.” This was validated by Vatican II’s explicit call for greater lay involvement in the Church, which led to changes such as the creation of parish councils and school boards on Long Island in the 1960s.

The result of all these changes was the cultural assimilation of immigrant ethnic communities within their various religions—the so-called “triple melting pot” model of Protestants, Catholics, and Jews. But while Koeth doesn’t emphasize this point, that assimilation also represented the cultural Protestantization of American Catholics. The absolute nuclear family was the dominant family structure of Anglo-Protestantism. Socioeconomic stratification was a hallmark of American Protestantism. Lay involvement was also a Protestant theme. Koeth quotes Andrew Greeley, a Catholic priest and sociologist, as saying that there was “little to distinguish the Catholic suburbanite from his Protestant neighbor.”

Another consequence of this assimilation was that conservative Catholics began to side with Protestants in culture-war disputes. Traditionally, Catholics had opposed prayer and Bible reading in public schools, viewing them as too Protestant and sectarian. But on Long Island, where many Catholics attended public schools, many began to favor nonsectarian school prayers. Jews, on the other hand, remained largely opposed—indeed, the lead plaintiff in a Long Island case in which the Supreme Court banned prayer in public schools was Jewish. This battle presaged future political shifts, with Jews remaining faithfully with the Democratic Party as some Catholics began migrating toward emerging conservative Republicanism, particularly in the post-Goldwater era.

Education proved another patch of treacherous terrain for the Catholic Church as Long Island suburbia abruptly matured. A range of forces made sustaining Catholic schools challenging in the new environment. A shortage of sisters deprived the Church of the supply of free teachers they had previously enjoyed, raising costs. High-quality public schools provided strong competition for parochial ones, while high property taxes to pay for those public schools squeezed the finances of Catholic families. Then, the end of the baby boom and the growth of empty-nest households created further headwinds. An initial shortage of seats in Catholic schools became a surplus, shuttering many. The diocese’s high schools never achieved their projected enrollments and were likewise forced into consolidation. Schools were just one symptom of overall suburban Catholic decline, as church attendance declines from 1950s highs mirrored those in America more broadly, and the Church began to struggle with questioning of its doctrines, such as a prohibition on contraception, by a restive, consumerist laity.

The Catholic Church’s mid-century plight on Long Island is indicative of the especially acute challenges faced by early suburban areas, both during their breakneck growth and afterward, when they were left with a housing stock heavy on homes too small for the tastes of later generations. Later suburbs, on the other hand, had advantages. They could draw on lessons learned from early suburbia. Their gentler growth curves meant an easier time scaling religious infrastructure with population growth. They had more variegated housing stock. (In many metro areas, early, inner-ring suburbs are suffering from significant decline and abandonment. This has not been the case on Long Island, where very restrictive zoning practices in the New York region have prevented newer exurbs from cannibalizing older suburbia, at the expense of rendering area housing markets very expensive).

Could a slower pace of suburbanization or better leadership in the Catholic Church have made the transition smoother? Perhaps, but it seems unlikely that the fundamental transformations wrought by suburbanization could have been avoided. Some type of ethnic assimilation was going to occur. Mass media was going to create a mass-consumerist culture. The very nature of suburbia inclines toward nuclear families, socioeconomic stratification, the dominance of public education, and central facilities reached by car. Just as it transformed American life generally, suburbanization was bound to transform American religion as well. Crabgrass Catholicism does a good job bringing that transformation to life for the Catholic world on Long Island.
